title: Subscription Renewal Propensity Modeling
summary: Built a renewal propensity model to identify customers most likely to respond to targeted mailing campaigns.
outcome: The model separates likely renewers from non-renewers with high reliability.
method: Feature engineering, model comparison, cross-validation, and variable-importance analysis.
contextLine: Business question: who should receive renewal offers first so campaign spend is not wasted?
howToRead: Each horizontal bar shows how strongly that factor influences renewal likelihood. Longer bars matter more.
interact: Hover over each bar to compare impact. Start from the longest bars to prioritize campaign segments.
conclusion: This told us exactly which customers are most likely to renew, mainly long-time subscribers and families with school-age kids. It helped the team focus offers where conversion is most probable, cut low-value outreach, and trust targeting decisions because model separation performance is strong.
detail: Representative project using anonymized (privacy-protected) customer and subscription attributes to optimize retention strategy.

title: Sports League Standings Engine
summary: Designed a standings workflow that converts match-level records into league-level performance metrics.
outcome: Generated sortable standings with points, pace, goal rates, and last-10 form.
method: Date filtering, home-away split processing, grouped aggregations, and ranking logic.
contextLine: Business question: how do we turn messy event logs into a ranking that people can act on quickly?
howToRead: Rows are ordered by points. Wins, draws, and losses explain how each team reached that rank.
interact: Scan the top rows first to identify leaders, then read down the table to spot immediate challengers.
conclusion: This told us who is truly performing once all matches are standardized into the same scoring logic. It helped convert raw results into a clear leaderboard, exactly like turning operational logs into an executive KPI ranking.
detail: Representative project with anonymized (privacy-protected) presentation mirroring production reporting needs.

title: Multi-Year Activity Analytics
summary: Analyzed long-term activity exports to quantify performance trends and workload across activity types.
outcome: Summarized 5,224.5 km of running and 57,278 m of climbing with cross-activity benchmarks.
method: Data preparation, grouped summaries, missing value handling, and comparative metrics.
contextLine: Business question: where does most of the effort happen so training time and planning stay aligned with reality?
howToRead: Compare slice sizes by activity to see where distance, climbing load, and activity volume concentrate.
interact: Switch between Distance, Climb, and Volume to view workload from three operational angles.
conclusion: This told us running drives most distance and elevation workload while other activities are complementary. It helped prioritize planning around the highest-impact activity instead of distributing effort evenly across lower-impact areas.
detail: Representative project presented with anonymized (privacy-protected) framing for operational fitness analytics.

title: Player Compensation and Ranking Analysis
summary: Connected ranking outcomes with salary structure to evaluate how top clubs allocate compensation.
outcome: Top-ranked clubs showed materially higher average salaries for attacking and midfield positions.
method: Pivot tables, grouped averages, top-tier segmentation, and comparative analysis.
contextLine: Business question: is compensation actually aligned with the roles that drive top-tier performance?
howToRead: For each position, compare Top 10 bars against Outside Top 10 bars to spot compensation gaps.
interact: Hover on each position group and compare differences role by role instead of overall averages.
conclusion: This told us top-ranked clubs pay a clear premium for attackers and midfielders, but not equally across all roles. It helped show where spend tracks competitive outcomes and where compensation strategy may be over- or under-weighted.
detail: Representative project using anonymized (privacy-protected) sports business data.

title: Visitor and Urban Visual Analytics
summary: Created visual narratives for location-based demand patterns and urban-scale indicators.
outcome: Highlighted distribution differences across geographies and trend direction by category.
method: Comparative plotting, segmentation, and hypothesis-oriented visual analysis.
contextLine: Business question: is demand spread evenly, or concentrated in a few regions that need priority attention?
howToRead: Each state is shaded by how many locations it has. Warmer, darker states have more locations and concentrated demand potential.
interact: Hover over each state and compare the most concentrated states first, then review the lighter, lower-volume states.
conclusion: This told us demand is concentrated in a small number of states rather than distributed evenly. It helped guide where to prioritize staffing, marketing, and operational focus instead of applying the same strategy everywhere.
detail: Representative project where charts focus on decision communication over raw output volume with anonymized (privacy-protected) context.

title: Library Operations SQL Suite
summary: Developed a broad SQL query set to answer catalog, usage, and circulation questions.
outcome: Delivered repeatable logic for borrower activity, popular titles, and inventory behavior.
method: Joins, aggregation, HAVING filters, and nested subqueries across relational entities.
contextLine: Business question: which catalog assets are actually used most so inventory and promotions stay relevant?
howToRead: Horizontal bars rank titles by checkout count. Longer bars indicate stronger demand and higher circulation value.
interact: Use the SQL scenario selector to switch question/query/result, then compare chart and table together.
conclusion: This told us exactly which titles attract the most borrowing activity. It helped focus stocking and promotion decisions on high-demand items while identifying where shelf space may be underperforming.
detail: Representative project framed with anonymized (privacy-protected) data patterns.

title: Retail Inventory and Sales SQL Analysis
summary: Modeled margin, supplier, and customer performance questions for a retail-style operation.
outcome: Surfaced high-impact insights on profitability, stock risk, and sales concentration.
method: Multi-table joins, TOP filters, period constraints, and union-based supplier views.
contextLine: Business question: which product categories deliver strong margins and which ones tie up inventory with low return?
howToRead: Bars compare average margin by category, while the overlaid line tracks the cumulative share. Taller bars indicate healthier revenue-to-cost performance.
interact: Hover each category to compare margin spread, follow the cumulative line to see how quickly margin concentrates, and cross-check with SQL output for root-cause detail.
conclusion: This told us not all categories contribute equally to profitability. It helped prioritize high-margin segments and flag low-return inventory where pricing or assortment changes are needed.
detail: Representative project with anonymized (privacy-protected) data for decision-support reporting.

title: Pricing and Fairness Econometric Analysis
summary: Assessed pricing/fine drivers using multivariate regression and interaction effects.
outcome: Quantified non-linear and interaction behavior affecting outcome magnitude across groups.
method: OLS baseline, controls, quadratic terms, and hypothesis testing.
contextLine: Business question: are pricing outcomes driven by one obvious factor, or by hidden non-linear patterns?
howToRead: The curve shows predicted outcome as age changes while other conditions are held constant.
interact: Move along the curve and compare slope changes to identify where pricing impact accelerates or softens.
conclusion: This told us pricing behavior is not linear and simple averages miss meaningful risk. It helped expose hidden pattern shifts so policy and pricing rules can be audited more fairly and adjusted with evidence.
detail: Representative analytical case with anonymized (privacy-protected) framing for policy-oriented modeling.

title: Foundations Portfolio
technique: Core data wrangling and analytical fundamentals
summary: Curated foundational exercises that established repeatable standards for later advanced projects.
outcome: Built strong capability in data wrangling, relational logic, and analytical communication.
method: Progressive implementation from core syntax to structured analytical workflows.
contextLine: Business question: do we have reliable fundamentals before scaling to advanced modeling and automation?
howToRead: Each spoke on the radar summarizes capability depth by area. Points farther from the center represent stronger implementation readiness.
interact: Hover each capability area to compare maturity and see where further improvement would add resilience.
conclusion: This told us the core workflow foundations are in place across Python, SQL, and R. It helped ensure advanced projects are built on stable, repeatable practices rather than one-off scripts.
detail: Representative foundational work that supports the advanced case studies shown above.
